content: Hyperuricemia is defined as serum urate level >6.8 mg/dL (approximately 7.0 mg/dL in men, >6.0 mg/dL in women), the saturation point above which monosodium urate crystals can form. Uric acid is the end product of purine metabolism in humans (lacking uricase enzyme). Hyperuricemia mechanisms: decreased renal excretion (90% - due to genetic variants in URAT1/SLC22A12, GLUT9/SLC2A9, ABCG2; CKD; medications like thiazides, loop diuretics, low-dose aspirin, cyclosporine, tacrolimus, niacin, pyrazinamide, ethambutol) or increased production (10% - high purine diet, alcohol, myeloproliferative disorders, tumor lysis syndrome, Lesch-Nyhan syndrome, psoriasis). Most individuals with hyperuricemia remain asymptomatic; only 20% develop gout. Associated conditions: metabolic syndrome, hypertension, CKD, cardiovascular disease, kidney stones (uric acid nephrolithiasis). Debate exists whether asymptomatic hyperuricemia should be treated.
